topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of old paper and strong coffee hung heavy in the air of the study. I found the laptop open on the desk, the screen illuminating a manuscript. My stomach tightened into a hard knot. It was *my* life, meticulously chronicled, sentence by agonizing sentence, by Mark. I leaned closer, my breath catching in my throat as I read a passage describing a mundane Tuesday evening—a description so clinical, so detached, it felt like reading a medical report about a stranger. My hands began to tremble, and I quickly pulled back, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. When Mark looked up, his expression was one of mild surprise, completely missing the seismic shift that had just occurred in the room.

[story 2]

I watched him type, the rhythmic click of the keys sounding alien in the quiet room. Every paragraph detailed my insecurities, my favorite brand of tea, the precise shade of blue in my eyes. A wave of nausea rolled over me, making my skin feel clammy. I stood by the doorway, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles were white, unable to move toward him or away from the scene. When he finally paused, rubbing his temples, and caught my stare, the look in his eyes was defensive, a flicker of panic that I found utterly repulsive. "What is it?" he asked, his voice too bright, too casual. I just shook my head slowly, a small, jerky movement, and turned abruptly, the sound of my own footsteps echoing hollowly.

[story 3]

The sheer audacity of it made my jaw ache. I sat on the edge of the sofa, my gaze fixed on the glowing screen where my own existence was being meticulously framed. It wasn't just writing; it was an excavation, a dissection of everything I was, reduced to narrative fodder. I felt a profound coldness seep into my bones, a chilling detachment from the warmth I usually felt around him. When he finally approached, offering a placating smile, I didn't look up. I just stared at the pattern of the text, focusing intently on the misplaced comma, my breathing shallow and fast. "It's… a lot," I managed to articulate, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. The silence that followed was thick, suffocating, punctuated only by the relentless tapping of the keyboard.
